full_response:
Step-by-step analysis of the game

1. Utilities  
   For Alice (symmetrically for Bob)  
   u_A(x_A , x_B)=  
   • 0                                                if x_A + x_B > 1  
   • x_A ( 1 − x_A − x_B )             if x_A + x_B ≤ 1.  

2. Candidate profile  
   (x_A , x_B) = (¼ , ¼).

3. Is Alice best-responding when Bob plays x_B = ¼ ?

   • Overload threshold with x_B = ¼ : Alice must choose x_A ≤ ¾ to avoid zero utility.

   • Within the feasible range [0 , ¾] Alice’s utility is  
     f(x_A)=x_A(1 − x_A − ¼)=x_A(¾ − x_A)= −x_A^2 + ¾ x_A.  
     This is a concave quadratic (opens downward), so its maximum is at the vertex:

          x_A^* = −(b)/(2a)   for  f(x)=ax^2+bx+c,  here  a = −1, b = ¾  
          ⇒  x_A^* = ¾ / 2 = 3/8 = 0.375.

   • The current action ¼ = 0.25 is strictly smaller than 3/8.  
     Value at ¼:     f(¼)=¼·(¾−¼)=¼·½ = ⅛ = 0.125.  
     Value at 3/8:  f(3/8)= (3/8)·(¾−3/8)=(3/8)·(3/8)=9/64 ≈ 0.140625.  
     Hence Alice can strictly increase her utility by moving from ¼ to 3/8.

   • Therefore Alice has a profitable deviation; (¼ , ¼) is not a best response for her.

4. Symmetry implies Bob can make the same deviation, but one profitable deviation already suffices to violate Nash equilibrium.

Conclusion  
(¼ , ¼) is not a Nash equilibrium, and the reason is that Alice can improve by increasing (not decreasing) her usage.

\boxed{\text{B. Not a Nash equilibrium since Alice can improve her utility by increasing her usage.}}

Confidence: 90%
extracted_answer: \text{B. Not a Nash equilibrium since Alice can improve her utility by increasing her usage.}
